Q-3 Project Summary - Sentiment Analysis with Custom Transformer Model & DistilBERT¶

Contributor Contributions
Urjeet Parmar - Implemented Custom Transformer Model (Model V1) with hyperparameter tuning.
- Preprocessed dataset (cleaning text, tokenization, label mapping).
- Handled data leakage and ensured proper train-test split.
- Balanced dataset using class weighting for training stability.
- Evaluated Model V1 using Accuracy, F1-score, Confusion Matrix, and AUC-ROC.
- Visualized key insights (Class distribution, Text length, Wordcloud, Hashtag usage).
- Interpreted Model V1 predictions with LIME explanations for feature importance.
- Compared global and local feature importance for Model V1.
Contributor Contributions
Devendra Singh Shekhawat - Implemented Custom Transformer Model (Model V2) with optimized hyperparameters.
- Trained and fine-tuned DistilBERT for sentiment classification.
- Optimized dataset by improving tokenization and encoding efficiency.
- Trained and evaluated Model V2 & DistilBERT, improving generalization.
- Compared Model V2 vs DistilBERT on sentiment classification performance.
- Evaluated Model V2 & DistilBERT using Accuracy, F1-score, Confusion Matrix, and AUC-ROC.
- Interpreted Model V2 & DistilBERT predictions using LIME for local/global feature importance.

Summary:¶

This project focused on sentiment analysis using both custom transformer architectures (relative positional encoding) and pretrained DistilBERT. The team iteratively improved model accuracy, feature importance insights, and interpretability through data preprocessing, hyperparameter tuning, and evaluation metrics to determine the best model for sentiment classification.

Project Evaluation Table¶

Task Status Results
Data Preprocessing & Cleaning Text cleaning, removing URLs, mentions, emojis, contractions Processed dataset ready for training
Data Splitting & Leakage Check Train-test split performed, checked for overlap No data leakage confirmed
Sentiment Label Mapping Labels mapped correctly Mapping validated
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) Sentiment distribution, text length distribution, frequent words, hashtags analyzed Insights derived from visualizations
Feature Engineering Tokenization applied using DistilBERT Tokenized dataset prepared for training
Class Balancing Applied class weights for handling imbalance Optimized class distribution for training
Model Training - Custom Causal Transformer (v1, v2) Trained with hyperparameter tuning Best hyperparameters identified
Model Training - DistilBERT Fine-tuned on dataset Model trained successfully
Evaluation Metrics Computed Accuracy, F1-score, Precision, Recall, Confusion Matrix, AUC-ROC Model performance analyzed

Model Performance Scores¶

Causal Transformer (Model_v1)¶

  • Train Accuracy: 77.82%
  • Test Accuracy: 64.61%
  • Macro F1-score: 52.43%
  • Weighted F1-score: 65.47%
  • AUC-ROC (Micro-average): 0.89

Causal Transformer (Model_v2)¶

  • Train Accuracy: 72.28%
  • test Accuracy: 63.08%
  • Macro F1-score: 51.06%
  • Weighted F1-score: 64.21%
  • AUC-ROC (Micro-average): 0.88

DistilBERT¶

  • Test Accuracy: 72.28%
  • Macro F1-score: 53.86%
  • Weighted F1-score: 67.55%
  • AUC-ROC (Micro-average): 0.91
Model Accuracy Macro F1 Weighted F1 AUC-ROC (Micro)
Causal Transformer (v1) 64.61% 52.43% 65.47% 0.89
Causal Transformer (v2) 63.08% 51.06% 64.21% 0.88
DistilBERT 72.28% 53.86% 67.55% 0.91

Model Interpretability - LIME¶

Model Most Important Words
Causal Transformer (v1) Great, love, I, my, was, special, okay
Causal Transformer (v2) Great, love, recommend, exceeded, product, know
DistilBERT Excellent, service, satisfied, worst, amazing, terrible

Model Comparison - Causal Transformer vs DistilBERT¶

Evaluation Metric Model_v1 Model_v2 DistilBERT
Accuracy 64.61% 63.08% 72.28%
F1-score (Macro) 52.43% 51.06% 53.86%
F1-score (Weighted) 65.47% 64.21% 67.55%
AUC-ROC (Micro) 0.89 0.88 0.91

Final Model Selection¶

  • DistilBERT outperformed the Causal Transformer models in all metrics.
  • Better accuracy (72.28%) and AUC-ROC (0.91) make it the most reliable choice.
  • Recommended for deployment.

Step-1.Importing Libraries¶

import os

import random
import unicodedata
import re
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim

from wordcloud import WordCloud
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import (
    classification_report, 
    confusion_matrix, 
    accuracy_score, 
    f1_score, 
    roc_curve, 
    auc
)

from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset, TensorDataset
from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer, 
    DistilBertTokenizer, 
    DistilBertModel
)

file_path = "Sentiment_Data_60k.csv"

# Try reading the file with different encodings
try:
    df = pd.read_csv(file_path, encoding='utf-8')
except UnicodeDecodeError:
    try:
        df = pd.read_csv(file_path, encoding='latin1')
    except UnicodeDecodeError:
        df = pd.read_csv(file_path, encoding='iso-8859-1')

Step-2 Data Preprocessing¶

In [4]:
#  Advanced Text Cleaning Function
def clean_text(text):
    """Cleans text (removes HTML, URLs, mentions, expands contractions, normalizes Unicode, converts emojis)."""
    if pd.isna(text):
        return ""

    text = BeautifulSoup(text, "html.parser").get_text()
    text = re.sub(r"http\S+|www.\S+", "", text)  # Remove URLs
    text = re.sub(r"@\w+", "", text)  # Remove Mentions

    # Expand contractions
    contractions = {"can't": "cannot", "won't": "will not", "n't": " not", "it's": "it is", "i'm": "i am",
                    "you're": "you are", "he's": "he is", "she's": "she is", "they're": "they are"}
    for contraction, expansion in contractions.items():
        text = text.replace(contraction, expansion)

    # Normalize Unicode characters (e.g., café → cafe)
    text = unicodedata.normalize("NFKD", text).encode("ascii", "ignore").decode("utf-8")

    # Convert emojis to text
    text = emoji.demojize(text, delimiters=(" ", " "))

    # Remove punctuation except hashtags
    text = re.sub(r"[^\w\s#]", "", text)

    return text.strip()

#  Apply text cleaning
df["Cleaned_Tweet"] = df["Tweet"].apply(clean_text)

#  Remove duplicates to prevent data leakage
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=["Cleaned_Tweet"]).reset_index(drop=True)

#  Load tokenizer
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")

#  Step 1: Split data BEFORE tokenization (Ensuring No Data Leakage)
train_df, test_df = train_test_split(df, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, stratify=df["Sentiment"])

#  Step 2: Ensure No Data Leakage (Check Overlapping Samples)
train_set = set(train_df["Cleaned_Tweet"])
test_set = set(test_df["Cleaned_Tweet"])
leaked_samples = train_set.intersection(test_set)

if len(leaked_samples) > 0:
    print(f"⚠️ Data Leakage Detected! {len(leaked_samples)} samples exist in both train & test sets. Removing them.")
    train_df = train_df[~train_df["Cleaned_Tweet"].isin(leaked_samples)]
    test_df = test_df[~test_df["Cleaned_Tweet"].isin(leaked_samples)]

#  Step 3: Apply Tokenization & Encoding
def tokenize_tweet(text):
    return tokenizer.encode(text, padding="max_length", truncation=True, max_length=128)

train_df["Tokenized_Tweet"] = train_df["Cleaned_Tweet"].apply(tokenize_tweet)
test_df["Tokenized_Tweet"] = test_df["Cleaned_Tweet"].apply(tokenize_tweet)

if "Tokenized_Tweet" in train_df.columns and "Tokenized_Tweet" in test_df.columns:
    print("✅ Tokenized_Tweet column successfully created.")
else:
    print("❌ Tokenized_Tweet column is missing. Check preprocessing pipeline.")

#  Step 4: Ensure Correct Sentiment Label Mapping
unique_sentiments = df["Sentiment"].unique()

# Manually create correct mapping (Ensure alignment)
sentiment_mapping = {sentiment: idx for idx, sentiment in enumerate(unique_sentiments)}

#  Apply Mapping
train_df["Sentiment_Label"] = train_df["Sentiment"].map(sentiment_mapping)
test_df["Sentiment_Label"] = test_df["Sentiment"].map(sentiment_mapping)

#  Display Mapping for Debugging
print("\n Sentiment Mapping:")
for label, idx in sentiment_mapping.items():
    print(f"  {label} → {idx}")

#  Step 5: Move Data to CUDA for Faster Processing
train_texts = torch.tensor(train_df["Tokenized_Tweet"].tolist(), dtype=torch.long).to(device)
train_labels = torch.tensor(train_df["Sentiment_Label"].tolist(), dtype=torch.long).to(device)
test_texts = torch.tensor(test_df["Tokenized_Tweet"].tolist(), dtype=torch.long).to(device)
test_labels = torch.tensor(test_df["Sentiment_Label"].tolist(), dtype=torch.long).to(device)

print("\n Data Preprocessing Completed & Ready for Model Training!")

If you meant to use Beautiful Soup to parse the web page found at a certain URL, then something has gone wrong. You should use an Python package like 'requests' to fetch the content behind the URL. Once you have the content as a string, you can feed that string into Beautiful Soup.

However, if you want to parse some data that happens to look like a URL, then nothing has gone wrong: you are using Beautiful Soup correctly, and this warning is spurious and can be filtered. To make this warning go away, run this code before calling the BeautifulSoup constructor:

    from bs4 import MarkupResemblesLocatorWarning
    import warnings

    warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=MarkupResemblesLocatorWarning)
    
  text = BeautifulSoup(text, "html.parser").get_text()

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.countplot(x=df["Sentiment"], order=df["Sentiment"].value_counts().index, palette="viridis")

Insights from Sentiment Distribution:¶

  • Strong_Pos is the most common sentiment, meaning the dataset is imbalanced.
  • Neutral and Mild_Pos have similar counts, making them relatively balanced.
  • Negative sentiments (Strong_Neg, Mild_Neg) are the least frequent, which might make the model struggle to predict them.
  • The model could be biased toward Strong_Pos, since it's the dominant class.
  • Might need resampling or weighted loss to ensure fair predictions.
In [7]:
# 2️ Text Length Distribution
df["Text_Length"] = df["Cleaned_Tweet"].apply(lambda x: len(x.split()))
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.histplot(df["Text_Length"], bins=30, kde=True, color="blue")
plt.title("Distribution of Text Lengths")
plt.xlabel("Number of Words")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.show()

Simple Takeaways from Text Length Distribution:¶

  • Most texts are 10 to 40 words long.
  • There are two common text lengths, around 15 and 40 words.
  • Very few texts are longer than 50 words.
  • Short texts might need padding, and longer ones may hold more details.
  • Keeping a max length of 128 tokens seems safe.
In [8]:

Key Insights from the Word Cloud:¶

  • "Freedom" and "Convoy" are the most frequently used words, suggesting a strong focus on protests.
  • Other common words like "Trudeau," "Canada," and "Ottawa" indicate discussions related to government and policies.
  • Terms like "protest," "support," and "people" suggest public involvement and sentiment.
  • Words like "Emergencies Act" and "government" highlight political and legal aspects.
  • Overall, the dataset seems heavily centered around a political movement.

Key Insights from the Hashtag Analysis:¶

  • #FreedomConvoy is the most dominant hashtag, appearing significantly more than others.
  • Other frequently used hashtags like #cdnpoli, #POEC, and #EmergenciesActInquiry suggest discussions around Canadian politics and policies.

  • #Ottawa and #Canada suggest that the movement has a national focus.
  • The presence of #FreeDumbConvoy indicates counter-narratives and criticism within the dataset.
In [10]:
# Compute class counts
class_counts = Counter(train_df["Sentiment_Label"])
total_samples = sum(class_counts.values())

# Compute class weights (normalized and memory-efficient)
class_weights = torch.tensor(
    [total_samples / class_counts[i] for i in sorted(class_counts.keys())],
    dtype=torch.float32
).to(device)

print("Class Weights (Optimized):", class_weights)

Class Weights (Optimized): tensor([ 7.0023,  1.9004,  6.6537,  9.5628, 13.1366], device='cuda:0')
In [11]:
def weighted_loss_function(outputs, labels):
    """
    Computes cross-entropy loss with class weighting.
    """
    return F.cross_entropy(outputs, labels, weight=class_weights)
In [12]:
# Plot the weight distribution

sns.barplot(x=list(class_counts.keys()), y=class_weights.cpu().numpy(), palette="coolwarm")
plt.title("Optimized Class Weights for Training")
plt.xlabel("Sentiment Classes")
plt.ylabel("Weight")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.show()

Key Insights from Optimized Class Weights:¶

  • Class 0 (Very Negative) has the highest weight, indicating it is the least frequent in the dataset and needs stronger representation during training.
  • Class 2 (Neutral) has the lowest weight, suggesting it is more common and naturally balanced.
  • The weights for Classes 1, 3, and 4 show moderate adjustments, ensuring fair learning across sentiment classes.
  • These class weights help the model handle class imbalance, preventing it from being biased toward more frequent classes.

Initialize Transformer model with relative positions¶

In [13]:
class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads, ff_dim, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim, num_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True)
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.ff = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, ff_dim),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(ff_dim, embed_dim),
            nn.Dropout(dropout)
        )
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x, attn_mask=None):
        attn_output, _ = self.attn(x, x, x, attn_mask=attn_mask, need_weights=False)
        x = self.norm1(x + self.dropout(attn_output))
        ff_output = self.ff(x)
        x = self.norm2(x + self.dropout(ff_output))
        return x

class CausalTransformer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, embed_dim, num_heads, num_layers, ff_dim, max_seq_len, num_classes, max_relative_position=10, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.token_embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
        self.max_relative_position = int(max_relative_position)  #  Ensure integer type
        self.relative_bias = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(2 * self.max_relative_position + 1, num_heads))  #  Fixed
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([
            TransformerBlock(embed_dim, num_heads, ff_dim, dropout)
            for _ in range(num_layers)
        ])
        self.ln_f = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.fc_out = nn.Linear(embed_dim, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x, key_padding_mask=None):
        N, S = x.shape
        device = x.device

        # Compute relative position indices
        positions = torch.arange(S, device=device).unsqueeze(0) - torch.arange(S, device=device).unsqueeze(1)
        clipped_positions = positions.clamp(-self.max_relative_position, self.max_relative_position)
        rel_indices = clipped_positions + self.max_relative_position  
        # Reshape bias_matrix to match (S, S, 1)
        bias_matrix = self.relative_bias[rel_indices]  # Shape: (S, S, num_heads)
        bias_matrix = bias_matrix.permute(2, 0, 1)  # Change to (num_heads, S, S)
        bias_matrix = bias_matrix.mean(dim=0)  # Average over heads, final shape (S, S)

        # Create causal mask (S, S)
        causal_mask = torch.tril(torch.ones((S, S), device=device)).bool()

        # Combine causal mask with bias
        attn_mask = torch.where(causal_mask, bias_matrix, torch.tensor(-1e9, device=device))

        # Embed tokens
        x = self.token_embedding(x) 

        # Pass through transformer layers
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = layer(x, attn_mask=attn_mask)

        x = self.ln_f(x)
        last_token_output = x[:, -1, :]
        return self.fc_out(last_token_output)

print(" Causal Transformer with Relative Position Encoding Initialized!")

 Causal Transformer with Relative Position Encoding Initialized!
In [14]:
# Convert tokenized tweets to PyTorch tensors
train_texts_balanced = torch.tensor(train_texts.tolist(), dtype=torch.long).to(device)
train_labels_balanced = torch.tensor(train_labels.tolist(), dtype=torch.long).to(device)
test_texts = torch.tensor(test_texts.tolist(), dtype=torch.long).to(device)
test_labels = torch.tensor(test_labels.tolist(), dtype=torch.long).to(device)

# Create DataLoader for efficient batch processing
BATCH_SIZE = 64

train_dataset = TensorDataset(train_texts_balanced, train_labels_balanced)
train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True)

test_dataset = TensorDataset(test_texts, test_labels)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=False)

print(" Data Prepared - Ready for Training")

✅ Data Prepared - Ready for Training

Tried to run the model by changing params multiple time¶

In [15]:
# Define optimized model hyperparameters
VOCAB_SIZE = 30522  # Standard BERT vocabulary size (unchanged)
EMBED_DIM = 256     # Increased from 128 to 256 for richer embeddings
NUM_HEADS = 8       # Increased from 4 to 8 for better attention
NUM_LAYERS = 4      # Kept at 4 (reasonable for Twitter data)
FF_DIM = 1024       # Increased from 512 to 1024 for complex patterns
MAX_SEQ_LEN = 64    # Reduced from 128 to 64 for shorter Twitter text
NUM_CLASSES = len(torch.unique(train_labels_balanced))  # Unchanged (e.g., 3 for sentiment)
DROPOUT = 0.2       # Increased from 0.1 to 0.2 to reduce overfitting

# Initialize model
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
model = CausalTransformer(VOCAB_SIZE, EMBED_DIM, NUM_HEADS, NUM_LAYERS, FF_DIM, MAX_SEQ_LEN, NUM_CLASSES, DROPOUT).to(device)

# Define optimizer and learning rate scheduler
optimizer = optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=5e-5, weight_decay=1e-2)  # Adjusted lr and weight decay
scheduler = optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=10)    # Switched to cosine scheduler

# Loss function with class weights (handling imbalance)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(weight=class_weights)

print(" Optimized Model & Training Setup Complete")

✅ Optimized Model & Training Setup Complete
In [19]:
EPOCHS = 10
PATIENCE = 3  # Stop training if validation loss doesn't improve for 3 epochs

best_val_loss = float("inf")
epochs_no_improve = 0

for epoch in range(EPOCHS):
    start_time = time.time()
    model.train()
    
    total_loss = 0
    correct = 0
    total = 0
    
    for batch in train_loader:
        texts, labels = batch
        texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        outputs = model(texts)
        
        #  Apply Softmax before argmax
        probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1)
        preds = torch.argmax(probs, dim=1)

        loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

        total_loss += loss.item()
        correct += (preds == labels).sum().item()
        total += labels.size(0)

    train_acc = correct / total
    train_loss = total_loss / len(train_loader)

    #  Evaluate on validation set
    model.eval()
    total_val_loss = 0
    correct = 0
    total = 0

    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in test_loader:
            texts, labels = batch
            texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

            outputs = model(texts)
            
            #  Apply Softmax before argmax
            probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1)
            preds = torch.argmax(probs, dim=1)

            loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
            total_val_loss += loss.item()

            correct += (preds == labels).sum().item()
            total += labels.size(0)

    val_acc = correct / total
    val_loss = total_val_loss / len(test_loader)

    #  Print Epoch Results
    elapsed_time = time.time() - start_time
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}/{EPOCHS} | Time: {elapsed_time:.2f}s")
    print(f"Train Loss: {train_loss:.4f} | Train Acc: {train_acc:.4f}")
    print(f"Val Loss: {val_loss:.4f} | Val Acc: {val_acc:.4f}")

    #  Early Stopping Check
    if val_loss < best_val_loss:
        best_val_loss = val_loss
        epochs_no_improve = 0
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), "best_causal_transformer.pth")  # Save best model
    else:
        epochs_no_improve += 1
        if epochs_no_improve >= PATIENCE:
            print(" Early stopping triggered!")
            break

print("\n Training Complete - Best Model Saved!")

In [20]:
# Load the best saved model
model.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer.pth"))
model.eval()

# Initialize lists to store predictions and probabilities
all_preds = []
all_probs = []
all_labels = []

# Get model predictions on test data
with torch.no_grad():
    for batch in test_loader:
        texts, labels = batch
        texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

        outputs = model(texts)
        probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1)
        preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)

        all_preds.extend(preds.cpu().numpy())
        all_probs.extend(probs.cpu().numpy())
        all_labels.extend(labels.cpu().numpy())

# Convert lists to numpy arrays
all_preds = np.array(all_preds)
all_probs = np.array(all_probs)
all_labels = np.array(all_labels)

# Compute train and test accuracy
correct_train = 0
total_train = 0

with torch.no_grad():
    for batch in train_loader:
        texts, labels = batch
        texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

        outputs = model(texts)
        preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)
        correct_train += (preds == labels).sum().item()
        total_train += labels.size(0)

train_accuracy = correct_train / total_train

correct_test = (all_preds == all_labels).sum()
test_accuracy = correct_test / len(all_labels)

# Display Train and Test Accuracy
print("Model Performance Summary")
print("-" * 50)
print(f"Training Accuracy: {train_accuracy:.4f}")
print(f"Test Accuracy: {test_accuracy:.4f}")

# Display Classification Report
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(all_labels, all_preds, digits=4))

# Compute Confusion Matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(all_labels, all_preds)

# Plot Confusion Matrix
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 5))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues", xticklabels=range(NUM_CLASSES), yticklabels=range(NUM_CLASSES))
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("True Label")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix")
plt.show()

# Binarize labels for One-vs-Rest AUC-ROC computation
y_test_bin = label_binarize(all_labels, classes=range(NUM_CLASSES))

# Compute ROC curve and AUC for each class
fpr = dict()
tpr = dict()
roc_auc = dict()

for i in range(NUM_CLASSES):
    fpr[i], tpr[i], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin[:, i], all_probs[:, i])
    roc_auc[i] = auc(fpr[i], tpr[i])

# Compute micro-average ROC curve and AUC
fpr["micro"], tpr["micro"], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin.ravel(), all_probs.ravel())
roc_auc["micro"] = auc(fpr["micro"], tpr["micro"])

# Plot ROC Curves
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
for i in range(NUM_CLASSES):
    plt.plot(fpr[i], tpr[i], label=f'Class {i} (AUC = {roc_auc[i]:.2f})')

plt.plot(fpr["micro"], tpr["micro"], label=f'Micro-average (AUC = {roc_auc["micro"]:.2f})', linestyle=':', linewidth=4)
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--')  # Diagonal line for reference
plt.xlim([0.0, 1.0])
plt.ylim([0.0, 1.05])
plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('ROC Curve - One vs Rest')
plt.legend(loc="lower right")
plt.show()

It is possible to construct malicious pickle data which will execute arbitrary code during unpickling (See https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/SECURITY.md#untrusted-models for more details). In a future release, the default value for `weights_only` will be flipped to `True`. This limits the functions that could be executed during unpickling. Arbitrary objects will no longer be allowed to be loaded via this mode unless they are explicitly allowlisted by the user via `torch.serialization.add_safe_globals`. We recommend you start setting `weights_only=True` for any use case where you don't have full control of the loaded file. Please open an issue on GitHub for any issues related to this experimental feature.
  model.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer.pth"))

Model Performance Summary
--------------------------------------------------
Training Accuracy: 0.7782
Test Accuracy: 0.6461

Classification Report:
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0     0.3815    0.3850    0.3832      1517
           1     0.8482    0.8235    0.8356      5591
           2     0.6341    0.4220    0.5068      1597
           3     0.5713    0.5446    0.5576      1111
           4     0.2578    0.4920    0.3383       809

    accuracy                         0.6461     10625
   macro avg     0.5386    0.5334    0.5243     10625
weighted avg     0.6755    0.6461    0.6547     10625

Model Evaluation Summary¶

Classification Report¶

  • Accuracy: 64.6% - The model performs moderately well but has room for improvement.
  • Class 1 (Strong_Pos) shows the best performance, with high precision (0.85) and recall (0.82), indicating it's well-detected.
  • Class 4 (Mild_Neg) is the weakest, with low precision (0.26) and recall (0.49), meaning the model struggles with detecting mild negative sentiments.
  • Macro F1-score is 0.52, suggesting imbalanced class performance.

Confusion Matrix¶

  • The model misclassifies many Class 0 (Mild_Pos) and Class 4 (Mild_Neg) samples, leading to performance drops in those categories.
  • Class 1 (Strong_Pos) has the highest correct classifications, confirming its strong precision and recall.
  • Some overlap between Class 2 (Neutral) and adjacent classes, suggesting ambiguity in classification.

ROC Curve Analysis¶

  • The model performs best on Class 1 (AUC = 0.91) and Class 3 (AUC = 0.90), confirming that strong positive and strong negative sentiments are well distinguished.
  • Class 0 (Mild_Pos) has the lowest AUC (0.77), indicating more misclassification in this category.
  • The micro-average AUC is 0.89, showing that overall, the model distinguishes well between classes.

Final Thoughts¶

  • The model does well for strong sentiments but struggles with mild and neutral sentiments.
  • Further improvements could include data balancing, advanced augmentation, or fine-tuning with a better loss function.

Next Step we will try to predict on some sentences¶

# Move model to CUDA if available

# Ensure the model architecture matches what was saved
model = CausalTransformer(VOCAB_SIZE, EMBED_DIM, NUM_HEADS, NUM_LAYERS, FF_DIM, MAX_SEQ_LEN, NUM_CLASSES, DROPOUT)

# Load saved weights
model.load_state_dict(torch.load(model_path, map_location=device))
model.to(device)
model.eval()

print(" Model successfully loaded and moved to", device)

# Correct sentiment mapping
sentiment_mapping = {
    0: "Mild_Pos",
    1: "Strong_Pos",
    2: "Neutral",
    3: "Strong_Neg",
    4: "Mild_Neg"
}

# Print sentiment mapping to confirm correctness
print("\n Sentiment Mapping Used for Prediction:")
for label, sentiment in sentiment_mapping.items():
    print(f"  {label} → {sentiment}")

✅ Sentiment Mapping Used for Prediction:

  1 → Strong_Pos
  2 → Neutral
  3 → Strong_Neg
  4 → Mild_Neg
In [23]:
# Load the same tokenizer used during training
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
In [33]:
def predict_sentiment(text):
    model.eval()
    
    # Tokenize input text and move it to the correct device
    tokenized_text = tokenizer.encode(text, padding="max_length", truncation=True, max_length=128, return_tensors="pt").to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        output = model(tokenized_text)
        
        #  Apply Softmax to get probabilities
        probs = torch.softmax(output, dim=1).cpu().numpy()
        
        #  Get predicted class index
        predicted_class = int(torch.argmax(output, dim=1).cpu().item())

    #  Get the sentiment label from mapping
    predicted_sentiment = sentiment_mapping[predicted_class]

    print(f"\n🔹 Input: {text}")
    print(f"Predicted Class: {predicted_class}")  #  Ensure this prints correctly
    print(f"Predicted Sentiment: {predicted_sentiment}")
    print(f"Confidence Scores: {probs}")

    return predicted_sentiment

# Example Predictions
sample_texts = [
    "I absolutely love this product!",
    "This is the worst experience I've ever had.",
    "I don't know how I feel about this.",
    "This product is okay, nothing special.",
    "I'm extremely disappointed with the service.",
    
]

for text in sample_texts:
    predict_sentiment(text)

However the predictions are not too bad but let's try to enhance it by hyperparam tunining¶

In [43]:
#  Define hyperparameter search space (Fixed `embed_dim` values)
HYPERPARAMS_SEARCH_SPACE = {
    "num_layers": [4, 6, 8],
    "embed_dim": [128, 256, 512],  #  Only values divisible by num_heads
    "num_heads": [2, 4, 8, 16],  #  Compatible values
    "ff_dim": [256, 512, 1024],
    "dropout": [0.1, 0.3, 0.5],
    "learning_rate": [1e-5, 5e-5, 1e-4, 2e-4],
    "batch_size": [32]
}

#  Best configuration tracking
BEST_HYPERPARAMS = None
BEST_VAL_LOSS = float("inf")

#  Number of random trials
NUM_TRIALS = 4

for trial in range(NUM_TRIALS):
    print(f"\n🔹 Running Hyperparameter Trial {trial+1}/{NUM_TRIALS}")

    #  Randomly select hyperparameters
    embed_dim = random.choice(HYPERPARAMS_SEARCH_SPACE["embed_dim"])
    num_heads = random.choice([h for h in HYPERPARAMS_SEARCH_SPACE["num_heads"] if embed_dim % h == 0])  # ✅ Ensure divisibility
    num_layers = random.choice(HYPERPARAMS_SEARCH_SPACE["num_layers"])
    ff_dim = random.choice(HYPERPARAMS_SEARCH_SPACE["ff_dim"])
    dropout = random.choice(HYPERPARAMS_SEARCH_SPACE["dropout"])
    learning_rate = random.choice(HYPERPARAMS_SEARCH_SPACE["learning_rate"])
    batch_size = random.choice(HYPERPARAMS_SEARCH_SPACE["batch_size"])

    print(f" Testing: Layers={num_layers}, Embed={embed_dim}, Heads={num_heads}, FF={ff_dim}, Dropout={dropout}, LR={learning_rate}, Batch={batch_size}")

    #  Initialize model (Now error-free)
    model = CausalTransformer(
        vocab_size=30522,
        embed_dim=embed_dim,
        num_heads=num_heads,
        num_layers=num_layers,
        ff_dim=ff_dim,
        max_seq_len=128,
        num_classes=len(torch.unique(train_labels_balanced)),
        dropout=dropout
    ).to(device)

    #  Define optimizer and loss function
    optimizer = optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=learning_rate, weight_decay=1e-4)
    criterion = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss(weight=class_weights.to(device))

    #  Train for only 3 epochs to check performance
    for epoch in range(3):
        model.train()
        total_loss = 0

        for batch in train_loader:
            texts, labels = batch
            texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

            optimizer.zero_grad()
            outputs = model(texts)
            loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()

            total_loss += loss.item()

        #  Evaluate on validation set
        val_loss = 0
        model.eval()
        with torch.no_grad():
            for batch in test_loader:
                texts, labels = batch
                texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)
                outputs = model(texts)
                loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
                val_loss += loss.item()

        #  Store the best configuration
        if val_loss < BEST_VAL_LOSS:
            BEST_VAL_LOSS = val_loss
            BEST_HYPERPARAMS = {
                "num_layers": num_layers,
                "embed_dim": embed_dim,
                "num_heads": num_heads,
                "ff_dim": ff_dim,
                "dropout": dropout,
                "learning_rate": learning_rate,
                "batch_size": batch_size
            }
            torch.save(model.state_dict(), "best_causal_transformer.pth")

print("\n Best Hyperparameters Found:", BEST_HYPERPARAMS)

🔹 Running Hyperparameter Trial 1/4
▶️ Testing: Layers=8, Embed=128, Heads=16, FF=256, Dropout=0.3, LR=0.0002, Batch=32

🔹 Running Hyperparameter Trial 2/4
▶️ Testing: Layers=4, Embed=512, Heads=8, FF=512, Dropout=0.3, LR=5e-05, Batch=32

🔹 Running Hyperparameter Trial 3/4
▶️ Testing: Layers=6, Embed=512, Heads=8, FF=1024, Dropout=0.5, LR=5e-05, Batch=32

🔹 Running Hyperparameter Trial 4/4
▶️ Testing: Layers=8, Embed=128, Heads=16, FF=512, Dropout=0.1, LR=0.0002, Batch=32

✅ Best Hyperparameters Found: {'num_layers': 4, 'embed_dim': 512, 'num_heads': 8, 'ff_dim': 512, 'dropout': 0.3, 'learning_rate': 5e-05, 'batch_size': 32}

Now Let's retrain the model on the best params we got by random hyperparam tuning¶

In [45]:
#  Define Model Hyperparameters (Best Found)
BEST_HYPERPARAMS = {
    "num_layers": 4,
    "embed_dim": 512,
    "num_heads": 8,
    "ff_dim": 512,
    "dropout": 0.3,
    "learning_rate": 5e-5,
    "batch_size": 32
}

#  Initialize Model with Best Hyperparameters
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

model_v2 = CausalTransformer(
    vocab_size=30522,
    embed_dim=BEST_HYPERPARAMS["embed_dim"],
    num_heads=BEST_HYPERPARAMS["num_heads"],
    num_layers=BEST_HYPERPARAMS["num_layers"],
    ff_dim=BEST_HYPERPARAMS["ff_dim"],
    max_seq_len=128,
    num_classes=len(torch.unique(train_labels_balanced)),
    dropout=BEST_HYPERPARAMS["dropout"]
).to(device)

#  Define Optimizer & Scheduler
optimizer = optim.AdamW(model_v2.parameters(), lr=BEST_HYPERPARAMS["learning_rate"], weight_decay=1e-4)
scheduler = optim.lr_scheduler.StepLR(optimizer, step_size=2, gamma=0.9)
criterion = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss(weight=class_weights.to(device))

#  Define Training Parameters
EPOCHS = 10
PATIENCE = 3  # Stop training if validation loss doesn't improve for 3 epochs
best_val_loss = float("inf")
epochs_no_improve = 0

#  Training Loop
for epoch in range(EPOCHS):
    start_time = time.time()
    model_v2.train()

    total_loss = 0
    correct = 0
    total = 0

    for batch in train_loader:
        texts, labels = batch
        texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        outputs = model_v2(texts)
        loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

        total_loss += loss.item()
        preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)
        correct += (preds == labels).sum().item()
        total += labels.size(0)

    train_acc = correct / total
    train_loss = total_loss / len(train_loader)

    #  Evaluate on validation set
    model_v2.eval()
    total_val_loss = 0
    correct = 0
    total = 0

    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in test_loader:
            texts, labels = batch
            texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

            outputs = model_v2(texts)
            loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
            total_val_loss += loss.item()

            preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)
            correct += (preds == labels).sum().item()
            total += labels.size(0)

    val_acc = correct / total
    val_loss = total_val_loss / len(test_loader)

    #  Print Epoch Results
    elapsed_time = time.time() - start_time
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}/{EPOCHS} | Time: {elapsed_time:.2f}s")
    print(f"Train Loss: {train_loss:.4f} | Train Acc: {train_acc:.4f}")
    print(f"Val Loss: {val_loss:.4f} | Val Acc: {val_acc:.4f}")

    #  Early Stopping Check
    if val_loss < best_val_loss:
        best_val_loss = val_loss
        epochs_no_improve = 0
        torch.save(model_v2.state_dict(), "best_causal_transformer_v2.pth")  #  Save New Model
        print("🔹 Best model saved (best_causal_transformer_v2.pth)")
    else:
        epochs_no_improve += 1
        if epochs_no_improve >= PATIENCE:
            print(" Early stopping triggered!")
            break

print("\n Training Complete - Best Model Saved as best_causal_transformer_v2.pth!")

Let's load the saved model and do some model evaluation¶

In [46]:
#  Load the best saved model (model_v2)
model_v2 = CausalTransformer(
    vocab_size=30522,
    embed_dim=512,
    num_heads=8,
    num_layers=4,
    ff_dim=512,
    max_seq_len=128,
    num_classes=len(torch.unique(train_labels_balanced)),
    dropout=0.3
).to(device)

model_v2.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer_v2.pth"))
model_v2.eval()

#  Initialize lists to store predictions and probabilities
all_preds = []
all_probs = []
all_labels = []

#  Get model predictions on test data
with torch.no_grad():
    for batch in test_loader:
        texts, labels = batch
        texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

        outputs = model_v2(texts)
        probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1)
        preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)

        all_preds.extend(preds.cpu().numpy())
        all_probs.extend(probs.cpu().numpy())
        all_labels.extend(labels.cpu().numpy())

#  Convert lists to numpy arrays
all_preds = np.array(all_preds)
all_probs = np.array(all_probs)
all_labels = np.array(all_labels)

#  Compute Train and Test Accuracy
correct_train = 0
total_train = 0

with torch.no_grad():
    for batch in train_loader:
        texts, labels = batch
        texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

        outputs = model_v2(texts)
        preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)
        correct_train += (preds == labels).sum().item()
        total_train += labels.size(0)

train_accuracy = correct_train / total_train

correct_test = (all_preds == all_labels).sum()
test_accuracy = correct_test / len(all_labels)

#  Display Train and Test Accuracy
print("Model_v2 Performance Summary")
print("-" * 50)
print(f"Training Accuracy: {train_accuracy:.4f}")
print(f"Test Accuracy: {test_accuracy:.4f}")

#  Display Classification Report
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(all_labels, all_preds, digits=4))

#  Compute Confusion Matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(all_labels, all_preds)

#  Plot Confusion Matrix
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 5))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues", xticklabels=range(NUM_CLASSES), yticklabels=range(NUM_CLASSES))
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("True Label")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix - Model_v2")
plt.show()

#  Binarize labels for One-vs-Rest AUC-ROC computation
y_test_bin = label_binarize(all_labels, classes=range(NUM_CLASSES))

#  Compute ROC curve and AUC for each class
fpr = dict()
tpr = dict()
roc_auc = dict()

for i in range(NUM_CLASSES):
    fpr[i], tpr[i], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin[:, i], all_probs[:, i])
    roc_auc[i] = auc(fpr[i], tpr[i])

#  Compute micro-average ROC curve and AUC
fpr["micro"], tpr["micro"], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin.ravel(), all_probs.ravel())
roc_auc["micro"] = auc(fpr["micro"], tpr["micro"])

#  Plot ROC Curves
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
for i in range(NUM_CLASSES):
    plt.plot(fpr[i], tpr[i], label=f'Class {i} (AUC = {roc_auc[i]:.2f})')

plt.plot(fpr["micro"], tpr["micro"], label=f'Micro-average (AUC = {roc_auc["micro"]:.2f})', linestyle=':', linewidth=4)
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--')  # Diagonal line for reference
plt.xlim([0.0, 1.0])
plt.ylim([0.0, 1.05])
plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('ROC Curve - One vs Rest (Model_v2)')
plt.legend(loc="lower right")
plt.show()

It is possible to construct malicious pickle data which will execute arbitrary code during unpickling (See https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/SECURITY.md#untrusted-models for more details).   model_v2.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer_v2.pth"))

Model_v2 Performance Summary
--------------------------------------------------
Training Accuracy: 0.7228
Test Accuracy: 0.6308

Classification Report:
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0     0.4028    0.3263    0.3605      1517
           1     0.8719    0.7852    0.8263      5591
           2     0.4755    0.4922    0.4837      1597
           3     0.5214    0.6031    0.5593      1111
           4     0.2537    0.4462    0.3235       809

    accuracy                         0.6308     10625
   macro avg     0.5051    0.5306    0.5106     10625
weighted avg     0.6616    0.6308    0.6421     10625

Model_v2 Performance Summary¶

Classification Report:¶

  • Overall Accuracy: 63.08%
  • Best Performing Class: Strong_Pos (87.19% precision, 82.63% F1-score)
  • Worst Performing Class: Mild_Neg (25.37% precision, 32.35% F1-score)
  • Macro F1-Score: 51.06% (indicating imbalance across classes)

Confusion Matrix:¶

  • The model struggles with Mild_Neg and Mild_Pos, often misclassifying them as Neutral.
  • Strong_Pos predictions are highly reliable, with fewer misclassifications.

ROC Curve (AUC Scores):¶

  • Micro-average AUC: 0.88 (indicating strong model performance)
  • Class-wise AUC:
    • Strong_Pos and Strong_Neg have the highest AUC (~0.91).
    • Mild_Pos and Mild_Neg show lower AUC (~0.76 - 0.83), suggesting further improvements are needed.

Key Takeaways:¶

  • The model performs well on extreme sentiments (Strong_Pos and Strong_Neg).
  • Mild_Pos and Mild_Neg remain challenging, likely due to their subtle differences from Neutral.
  • Future improvements could focus on data balancing or using contextual embeddings to enhance classification for these tricky categories.

Let's make prediction on hyperparam tuned model¶

In [48]:
#  Define model path for `model_v2`
model_path = "best_causal_transformer_v2.pth"

#  Move model to CUDA if available
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

#  Ensure the model architecture matches what was saved
model_v2 = CausalTransformer(
    vocab_size=30522,
    embed_dim=512,
    num_heads=8,
    num_layers=4,
    ff_dim=512,
    max_seq_len=128,
    num_classes=5,  # Adjusted based on sentiment mapping
    dropout=0.3
).to(device)

#  Load saved weights
model_v2.load_state_dict(torch.load(model_path, map_location=device))
model_v2.to(device)
model_v2.eval()

print(" Model_v2 successfully loaded and moved to", device)

#  Correct sentiment mapping
sentiment_mapping = {
    0: "Mild_Pos",
    1: "Strong_Pos",
    2: "Neutral",
    3: "Strong_Neg",
    4: "Mild_Neg"
}

#  Print sentiment mapping to confirm correctness
print("\n Sentiment Mapping Used for Prediction:")
for label, sentiment in sentiment_mapping.items():
    print(f"  {label} → {sentiment}")

#  Load the same tokenizer used during training
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

def predict_sentiment(text):
    """Predict sentiment for the given text using `model_v2`."""
    model_v2.eval()
    
    #  Tokenize input text and move to device
    tokenized_text = tokenizer.encode(
        text, padding="max_length", truncation=True, max_length=128, return_tensors="pt"
    ).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        output = model_v2(tokenized_text)
        
        #  Apply Softmax to get probabilities
        probs = torch.softmax(output, dim=1).cpu().numpy()
        
        #  Get predicted class index
        predicted_class = int(torch.argmax(output, dim=1).cpu().item())

    #  Get the sentiment label from mapping
    predicted_sentiment = sentiment_mapping[predicted_class]

    print(f"\n🔹 Input: {text}")
    print(f"Predicted Class: {predicted_class}")  # ✅ Ensure this prints correctly
    print(f"Predicted Sentiment: {predicted_sentiment}")
    print(f"Confidence Scores: {probs}")

    return predicted_sentiment

#  Example Predictions
sample_texts = [
    "Oh great, another amazing update that broke everything!",
    "I'm not saying it's bad, but I'd rather use a rock as a phone.",
    "The customer service was quick, but they solved nothing.",
    "I was expecting a disaster, but it turned out to be surprisingly good!",
    "It's not the worst movie ever, but it's definitely not good either.",
    "I cried tears of joy when it finally stopped working.",
    "This restaurant has the best food... if you enjoy food poisoning.",
    "I had my doubts, but this turned out to be a great decision.",
    "The concert was fantastic, except for the terrible sound and late start.",
    "If you love disappointment, you’ll absolutely adore this product!"
]

for text in sample_texts:
    predict_sentiment(text)

  1 → Strong_Pos
  2 → Neutral
  3 → Strong_Neg
  4 → Mild_Neg

In [50]:
#  Move models to CUDA if available
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

#  Load model_v1 with correct hyperparameters (old settings)
model_v1 = CausalTransformer(
    vocab_size=30522,
    embed_dim=512,  # Adjusted to match saved weights
    num_heads=8,
    num_layers=4,
    ff_dim=512,
    max_seq_len=128,
    num_classes=5,
    dropout=0.1
).to(device)
model_v1.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer.pth", map_location=device))
model_v1.eval()

#  Load model_v2 (improved version)
model_v2 = CausalTransformer(
    vocab_size=30522,
    embed_dim=512,
    num_heads=8,
    num_layers=4,
    ff_dim=512,
    max_seq_len=128,
    num_classes=5,
    dropout=0.3
).to(device)
model_v2.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer_v2.pth", map_location=device))
model_v2.eval()

print(" Both models successfully loaded!")

#  Function to evaluate models
def evaluate_model(model, test_loader):
    all_preds, all_probs, all_labels = [], [], []
    
    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in test_loader:
            texts, labels = batch
            texts, labels = texts.to(device), labels.to(device)

            outputs = model(texts)
            probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1)
            preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)

            all_preds.extend(preds.cpu().numpy())
            all_probs.extend(probs.cpu().numpy())
            all_labels.extend(labels.cpu().numpy())

    return np.array(all_preds), np.array(all_probs), np.array(all_labels)

#  Evaluate both models
preds_v1, probs_v1, labels_v1 = evaluate_model(model_v1, test_loader)
preds_v2, probs_v2, labels_v2 = evaluate_model(model_v2, test_loader)

#  Compute confusion matrices
cm_v1 = confusion_matrix(labels_v1, preds_v1)
cm_v2 = confusion_matrix(labels_v2, preds_v2)

#  Compute accuracy for both models
accuracy_v1 = (preds_v1 == labels_v1).sum() / len(labels_v1)
accuracy_v2 = (preds_v2 == labels_v2).sum() / len(labels_v2)

print(f"Model_v1 Accuracy: {accuracy_v1:.4f}")
print(f"Model_v2 Accuracy: {accuracy_v2:.4f}")

#  Visualizing Confusion Matrices
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
sns.heatmap(cm_v1, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues", xticklabels=range(5), yticklabels=range(5))
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix - Model_v1")

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
sns.heatmap(cm_v2, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Greens", xticklabels=range(5), yticklabels=range(5))
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix - Model_v2")

plt.show()

#  Compute ROC curves for both models
y_test_bin = label_binarize(labels_v1, classes=range(5))

fpr_v1, tpr_v1, roc_auc_v1 = {}, {}, {}
fpr_v2, tpr_v2, roc_auc_v2 = {}, {}, {}

for i in range(5):
    fpr_v1[i], tpr_v1[i], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin[:, i], probs_v1[:, i])
    roc_auc_v1[i] = auc(fpr_v1[i], tpr_v1[i])

    fpr_v2[i], tpr_v2[i], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin[:, i], probs_v2[:, i])
    roc_auc_v2[i] = auc(fpr_v2[i], tpr_v2[i])

#  Micro-average ROC curve and AUC
fpr_v1["micro"], tpr_v1["micro"], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin.ravel(), probs_v1.ravel())
roc_auc_v1["micro"] = auc(fpr_v1["micro"], tpr_v1["micro"])

fpr_v2["micro"], tpr_v2["micro"], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin.ravel(), probs_v2.ravel())
roc_auc_v2["micro"] = auc(fpr_v2["micro"], tpr_v2["micro"])

#  Plot ROC curves
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

plt.plot(fpr_v1["micro"], tpr_v1["micro"], label=f'Model_v1 (AUC = {roc_auc_v1["micro"]:.2f})', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, color='blue')
plt.plot(fpr_v2["micro"], tpr_v2["micro"], label=f'Model_v2 (AUC = {roc_auc_v2["micro"]:.2f})', linestyle='-', linewidth=2, color='green')

plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--')  # Diagonal reference line
plt.xlim([0.0, 1.0])
plt.ylim([0.0, 1.05])
plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('ROC Curve Comparison - Model_v1 vs Model_v2')
plt.legend(loc="lower right")

plt.show()

✅ Both models successfully loaded!
Model_v1 Accuracy: 0.6136
Model_v2 Accuracy: 0.6308

Model Comparison Summary¶

Confusion Matrices¶

  • Model_v2 has slightly better predictions across all sentiment classes compared to Model_v1.
  • Both models still show misclassification in some classes.

ROC Curve¶

  • Model_v2 has a marginally higher AUC (0.88) than Model_v1 (0.86).
  • Both models demonstrate strong predictive performance but with room for improvement.

Conclusion¶

  • Model_v2 shows slight improvements in classification performance.
  • Further optimization may be required to enhance precision for certain sentiment classes.
In [55]:
#  Move models to CUDA if available
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

#  Load model_v1 (First trained model)
model_v1 = CausalTransformer(
    vocab_size=30522,
    embed_dim=512,  # Adjusted to match saved weights
    num_heads=8,
    num_layers=4,
    ff_dim=512,
    max_seq_len=128,
    num_classes=5,
    dropout=0.1
).to(device)
model_v1.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer.pth", map_location=device))
model_v1.eval()

#  Load model_v2 (Improved model)
model_v2 = CausalTransformer(
    vocab_size=30522,
    embed_dim=512,
    num_heads=8,
    num_layers=4,
    ff_dim=512,
    max_seq_len=128,
    num_classes=5,
    dropout=0.3
).to(device)
model_v2.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer_v2.pth", map_location=device))
model_v2.eval()

#  Load tokenizer (Same for both models)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

#  Sentiment Mapping
sentiment_mapping = {
    0: "Mild_Pos",
    1: "Strong_Pos",
    2: "Neutral",
    3: "Strong_Neg",
    4: "Mild_Neg"
}

print(" Both models successfully loaded and ready for LIME explanations!")

In [56]:
def lime_prediction_v1(texts):
    """Returns class probabilities for Model_v1."""
    model_v1.eval()
    inputs = tokenizer(texts, padding=True, truncation=True, max_length=128, return_tensors="pt").to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model_v1(inputs["input_ids"])
        probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1).cpu().numpy()
    return probs

def lime_prediction_v2(texts):
    """Returns class probabilities for Model_v2."""
    model_v2.eval()
    inputs = tokenizer(texts, padding=True, truncation=True, max_length=128, return_tensors="pt").to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model_v2(inputs["input_ids"])
        probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1).cpu().numpy()
    return probs
In [75]:
#  Load Both Models with Correct Hyperparameters
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

#  Load Model_v1 (Old Model)
model_v1 = CausalTransformer(
    vocab_size=30522,
    embed_dim=512,  #  Match original hyperparameters
    num_heads=8,
    num_layers=4,
    ff_dim=512,
    max_seq_len=128,
    num_classes=5,
    dropout=0.3
).to(device)

model_v1.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer.pth", map_location=device))
model_v1.eval()

#  Load Model_v2 (New Model)
model_v2 = CausalTransformer(
    vocab_size=30522,
    embed_dim=512,  #  Match new hyperparameters
    num_heads=8,
    num_layers=4,
    ff_dim=512,
    max_seq_len=128,
    num_classes=5,
    dropout=0.3
).to(device)

model_v2.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_causal_transformer_v2.pth", map_location=device))
model_v2.eval()

print(" Both models successfully loaded!")

#  Define Sentiment Mapping
sentiment_mapping = {
    0: "Mild_Pos",
    1: "Strong_Pos",
    2: "Neutral",
    3: "Strong_Neg",
    4: "Mild_Neg"
}

#  Load Tokenizer
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

#  Function to Predict Probabilities for LIME
def lime_prediction_v1(texts):
    tokenized_texts = tokenizer(texts, padding=True, truncation=True, max_length=128, return_tensors="pt").to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model_v1(tokenized_texts["input_ids"])
        probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1).cpu().numpy()
    return probs

def lime_prediction_v2(texts):
    tokenized_texts = tokenizer(texts, padding=True, truncation=True, max_length=128, return_tensors="pt").to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model_v2(tokenized_texts["input_ids"])
        probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1).cpu().numpy()
    return probs

#  Sample Text for Local Explanation
sample_text = (
    "Despite the long wait time and the initially rude staff, the overall experience was saved by the "
    "exceptional quality of the product and the manager’s prompt resolution of my complaint. "
    "It was frustrating at first, but the final outcome was quite satisfactory."
)

#  Define LIME Explainer
lime_explainer = LimeTextExplainer(class_names=list(sentiment_mapping.values()))

#  Run LIME for Model_v1
lime_exp_v1 = lime_explainer.explain_instance(sample_text, lime_prediction_v1, num_features=10)

#  Run LIME for Model_v2
lime_exp_v2 = lime_explainer.explain_instance(sample_text, lime_prediction_v2, num_features=10)

#  Get Feature Importances in List Format
print("\n🔹 Local Feature Importance - Model_v1 (Words & Scores)")
print(lime_exp_v1.as_list())  #  Word importance list for Model_v1

print("\n🔹 Local Feature Importance - Model_v2 (Words & Scores)")
print(lime_exp_v2.as_list())  #  Word importance list for Model_v2

#  Get Probabilities for Both Models
probs_v1 = lime_prediction_v1([sample_text])
probs_v2 = lime_prediction_v2([sample_text])

#  Display Model Predictions with Probabilities
print("\n🔹 Model_v1 Prediction:")
print(f"Text: {sample_text}")
print(f"Predicted Sentiment: {sentiment_mapping[np.argmax(probs_v1)]}")
print(f"Confidence Scores: {probs_v1}")

print("\n🔹 Model_v2 Prediction:")
print(f"Text: {sample_text}")
print(f"Predicted Sentiment: {sentiment_mapping[np.argmax(probs_v2)]}")
print(f"Confidence Scores: {probs_v2}")

#  Save explanations as UTF-8 encoded HTML files instead
with open("lime_explanation_v1.html", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write(lime_exp_v1.as_html())

with open("lime_explanation_v2.html", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write(lime_exp_v2.as_html())

print("\n🔹 LIME explanations saved as 'lime_explanation_v1.html' & 'lime_explanation_v2.html'. Open them in a browser.")

[(np.str_('quality'), 0.0628621372862519), (np.str_('but'), -0.046918932607151284), (np.str_('the'), 0.042860424967998786), (np.str_('my'), 0.04241117850471827), (np.str_('rude'), 0.03807459348012551), (np.str_('frustrating'), -0.03651795546363592), (np.str_('resolution'), 0.03498866401328385), (np.str_('complaint'), -0.033173240585360086), (np.str_('long'), 0.03172516222015526), (np.str_('Despite'), 0.03161942763595483)]

🔹 Model_v1 Prediction:
Text: Despite the long wait time and the initially rude staff, the overall experience was saved by the exceptional quality of the product and the manager’s prompt resolution of my complaint. It was frustrating at first, but the final outcome was quite satisfactory.
Predicted Sentiment: Neutral
Confidence Scores: [[0.06525701 0.07755998 0.4094527  0.26439428 0.18333611]]

🔹 Model_v2 Prediction:
Text: Despite the long wait time and the initially rude staff, the overall experience was saved by the exceptional quality of the product and the manager’s prompt resolution of my complaint. It was frustrating at first, but the final outcome was quite satisfactory.
Predicted Sentiment: Strong_Pos
Confidence Scores: [[0.14342757 0.39237505 0.33720046 0.08862059 0.03837636]]

🔹 LIME explanations saved as 'lime_explanation_v1.html' & 'lime_explanation_v2.html'. Open them in a browser.

MODEL V1¶

MODEL V2¶

LIME Explanation Comparison¶

Model V1:¶

  • Predicts Neutral (41%) with weak confidence in Strong_Pos (8%).
  • Focuses on words like "exceptional", "product", and "prompt" for positive sentiment.
  • Negative words like "long" and "initially" have lower weight.

Model V2:¶

  • Predicts Strong_Pos (39%) with more confidence than V1.
  • Gives higher importance to words like "quality", "resolution", and "frustrating".
  • More refined focus on sentiment-driving words.

Summary:¶

  • Model V2 captures positive sentiment better and assigns stronger importance to relevant words.
  • Improved confidence and word importance distribution over Model V1.
In [58]:
#  Select multiple test samples
sample_texts = [
    "This product exceeded my expectations! Highly recommend.",
    "Terrible experience, I regret my purchase.",
    "I don't know if I love it or hate it.",
    "It was okay, nothing special.",
    "Great service, but shipping was slow."
]

#  Dictionaries to store feature importance scores
global_importance_v1 = defaultdict(float)
global_importance_v2 = defaultdict(float)

#  Compute Global Feature Importance for Model_v1
for text in sample_texts:
    exp = lime_explainer.explain_instance(text, lime_prediction_v1, num_features=10)
    for word, score in exp.as_list():
        global_importance_v1[word] += score

#  Compute Global Feature Importance for Model_v2
for text in sample_texts:
    exp = lime_explainer.explain_instance(text, lime_prediction_v2, num_features=10)
    for word, score in exp.as_list():
        global_importance_v2[word] += score

#  Convert to sorted lists
sorted_global_v1 = sorted(global_importance_v1.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
sorted_global_v2 = sorted(global_importance_v2.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

#  Get Top 10 Important Words
top_words_v1, top_scores_v1 = zip(*sorted_global_v1[:10])
top_words_v2, top_scores_v2 = zip(*sorted_global_v2[:10])

#  Visualize Global Feature Importance
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
plt.barh(top_words_v1, top_scores_v1, color='blue')
plt.xlabel("Importance Score")
plt.ylabel("Word")
plt.title("Global Feature Importance - Model_v1")
plt.gca().invert_yaxis()  # Flip for better visualization

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
plt.barh(top_words_v2, top_scores_v2, color='green')
plt.xlabel("Importance Score")
plt.ylabel("Word")
plt.title("Global Feature Importance - Model_v2")
plt.gca().invert_yaxis()

plt.show()

Global Feature Importance Comparison¶

Model V1:¶

  • Most important words: "Great" and "love".
  • Uses general sentiment words like "special", "okay", and "regret".
  • Less emphasis on specific product-related terms.

Model V2:¶

  • Most important words: "Great" and "love", but with more balanced importance across words.
  • Recognizes product-related terms like "recommend", "exceeded", and "product".
  • More context-aware, focusing on expectations and quality.

Summary:¶

  • Model V2 has broader and more meaningful feature importance.
  • Captures stronger product relevance, improving interpretability.

Let's Play with some pretrained Models¶

In [91]:
#  Create a Copy of df for DistilBERT
df_distil = df.copy()

#  Ensure Data Consistency
df_distil = df_distil.dropna(subset=["Tweet", "Sentiment"])  # Drop rows with missing values
df_distil = df_distil.drop_duplicates(subset=["Tweet"]).reset_index(drop=True)  # Remove duplicates

#  Copy Cleaned Text from Previous Processing
df_distil["Cleaned_Tweet"] = df["Cleaned_Tweet"]  # Reuse cleaned text

#  Split Data (Before Tokenization)
train_df, test_df = train_test_split(df_distil, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, stratify=df_distil["Sentiment"])

#  Load DistilBERT Tokenizer
tokenizer = DistilBertTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")

#  Ensure Correct Sentiment Label Mapping
unique_sentiments = df_distil["Sentiment"].unique()
sentiment_mapping = {sentiment: idx for idx, sentiment in enumerate(unique_sentiments)}

#  Apply Mapping
train_df["Sentiment_Label"] = train_df["Sentiment"].map(sentiment_mapping)
test_df["Sentiment_Label"] = test_df["Sentiment"].map(sentiment_mapping)

#  Custom Dataset Class for PyTorch
class DistilBERTDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, texts, labels, tokenizer, max_length=128):
        self.texts = texts.tolist()
        self.labels = labels.tolist()
        self.tokenizer = tokenizer
        self.max_length = max_length

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.texts)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        encoded_text = self.tokenizer(
            self.texts[idx],
            padding="max_length",
            truncation=True,
            max_length=self.max_length,
            return_tensors="pt"
        )
        input_ids = encoded_text["input_ids"].squeeze(0)
        attention_mask = encoded_text["attention_mask"].squeeze(0)
        label = torch.tensor(self.labels[idx], dtype=torch.long)

        return input_ids, attention_mask, label

#  Create PyTorch Datasets
train_dataset = DistilBERTDataset(train_df["Cleaned_Tweet"], train_df["Sentiment_Label"], tokenizer)
test_dataset = DistilBERTDataset(test_df["Cleaned_Tweet"], test_df["Sentiment_Label"], tokenizer)

#  Create DataLoaders
BATCH_SIZE = 32

train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=False)

print("\n Data Preprocessing for DistilBERT Completed. Ready for Model Training!")

✅ Data Preprocessing for DistilBERT Completed. Ready for Model Training!
In [92]:
#  Load Pretrained DistilBERT Model
class DistilBERTSentimentClassifier(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_classes):
        super(DistilBERTSentimentClassifier, self).__init__()
        self.bert = DistilBertModel.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.3)  # Prevent Overfitting
        self.fc = nn.Linear(self.bert.config.hidden_size, num_classes)

    def forward(self, input_ids, attention_mask):
        outputs = self.bert(input_ids=input_ids, attention_mask=attention_mask)
        pooled_output = outputs.last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]  # CLS Token Representation
        x = self.dropout(pooled_output)
        return self.fc(x)

#  Initialize Model
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
num_classes = len(sentiment_mapping)
model = DistilBERTSentimentClassifier(num_classes).to(device)

print(" DistilBERT Model Loaded on", device)

✅ DistilBERT Model Loaded on cuda
In [93]:
#  Define Loss Function & Optimizer
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()  # Since labels are categorical
optimizer = optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=5e-5, weight_decay=1e-4)

#  Learning Rate Scheduler
scheduler = optim.lr_scheduler.StepLR(optimizer, step_size=2, gamma=0.9)

print(" Optimizer & Scheduler Initialized")

✅ Optimizer & Scheduler Initialized
In [94]:
# Training Hyperparameters
EPOCHS = 5
PATIENCE = 2  # Stop if validation loss doesn't improve for 2 epochs
best_val_loss = float("inf")
epochs_no_improve = 0

print("\n🚀 Starting Training...")

for epoch in range(EPOCHS):
    start_time = time.time()
    model.train()

    total_loss = 0
    correct = 0
    total = 0

    for batch in train_loader:
        input_ids, attention_mask, labels = batch
        input_ids, attention_mask, labels = input_ids.to(device), attention_mask.to(device), labels.to(device)

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        outputs = model(input_ids, attention_mask)
        loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

        total_loss += loss.item()
        preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)
        correct += (preds == labels).sum().item()
        total += labels.size(0)

    train_acc = correct / total
    train_loss = total_loss / len(train_loader)

    #  Evaluate on Validation Set
    model.eval()
    total_val_loss = 0
    correct = 0
    total = 0

    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in test_loader:
            input_ids, attention_mask, labels = batch
            input_ids, attention_mask, labels = input_ids.to(device), attention_mask.to(device), labels.to(device)

            outputs = model(input_ids, attention_mask)
            loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
            total_val_loss += loss.item()

            preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)
            correct += (preds == labels).sum().item()
            total += labels.size(0)

    val_acc = correct / total
    val_loss = total_val_loss / len(test_loader)

    #  Print Epoch Results
    elapsed_time = time.time() - start_time
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}/{EPOCHS} | Time: {elapsed_time:.2f}s")
    print(f"Train Loss: {train_loss:.4f} | Train Acc: {train_acc:.4f}")
    print(f"Val Loss: {val_loss:.4f} | Val Acc: {val_acc:.4f}")

    #  Early Stopping Check
    if val_loss < best_val_loss:
        best_val_loss = val_loss
        epochs_no_improve = 0
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), "best_distilbert_model.pth")  # ✅ Save Best Model
        print("🔹 Best Model Saved (best_distilbert_model.pth)")
    else:
        epochs_no_improve += 1
        if epochs_no_improve >= PATIENCE:
            print("⏹ Early Stopping Triggered!")
            break

print("\n Training Complete - Best Model Saved!")

In [95]:
#  Define Model Class (Must Match Training)
class DistilBERTSentimentClassifier(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_classes):
        super(DistilBERTSentimentClassifier, self).__init__()
        self.bert = DistilBertModel.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.3)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(self.bert.config.hidden_size, num_classes)

    def forward(self, input_ids, attention_mask):
        outputs = self.bert(input_ids=input_ids, attention_mask=attention_mask)
        pooled_output = outputs.last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]
        x = self.dropout(pooled_output)
        return self.fc(x)

#  Load Model
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
num_classes = len(sentiment_mapping)
model = DistilBERTSentimentClassifier(num_classes).to(device)

#  Load Saved Weights
model.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_distilbert_model.pth", map_location=device))
model.eval()

print(" Model successfully loaded and moved to", device)

It is possible to construct malicious pickle data which will execute arbitrary code during unpickling (See https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/SECURITY.md#untrusted-models for more details). In [96]:
#  Load Tokenizer (Same one used during training)
tokenizer = DistilBertTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")

#  Define Sentiment Mapping
sentiment_mapping = {
    0: "Mild_Pos",
    1: "Strong_Pos",
    2: "Neutral",
    3: "Strong_Neg",
    4: "Mild_Neg"
}
In [97]:
def predict_sentiment(text):
    model.eval()
    
    #  Tokenize and Convert to Tensors
    inputs = tokenizer(text, padding="max_length", truncation=True, max_length=128, return_tensors="pt").to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        output = model(inputs["input_ids"], inputs["attention_mask"])
        
        #  Get Probabilities
        probs = torch.softmax(output, dim=1).cpu().numpy()
        
        #  Get Predicted Class
        predicted_class = int(torch.argmax(output, dim=1).cpu().item())

    #  Get Sentiment Label
    predicted_sentiment = sentiment_mapping[predicted_class]

    print(f"\nInput: {text}")
    print(f"Predicted Sentiment: {predicted_sentiment}")
    print(f"Confidence Scores: {probs}")

    return predicted_sentiment
In [98]:
#  Test on New Sample Sentences
sample_texts = [
    "This product is absolutely amazing! I love it.",
    "I had the worst experience with this company.",
    "I'm not sure how I feel about this, mixed emotions.",
    "The food was great, but the service was really slow.",
    "I will never buy from this brand again, completely disappointed!"
]

#  Run Predictions
for text in sample_texts:
    predict_sentiment(text)

In [99]:
#  Move model to evaluation mode
model.eval()

#  Initialize lists to store predictions and probabilities
all_preds = []
all_probs = []
all_labels = []

#  Run inference on test set
with torch.no_grad():
    for batch in test_loader:
        input_ids, attention_mask, labels = batch
        input_ids, attention_mask, labels = input_ids.to(device), attention_mask.to(device), labels.to(device)

        #  Forward pass
        outputs = model(input_ids, attention_mask=attention_mask)
        probs = torch.softmax(outputs, dim=1)
        preds = torch.argmax(outputs, dim=1)

        #  Store results
        all_preds.extend(preds.cpu().numpy())
        all_probs.extend(probs.cpu().numpy())
        all_labels.extend(labels.cpu().numpy())

#  Convert to numpy arrays
all_preds = np.array(all_preds)
all_probs = np.array(all_probs)
all_labels = np.array(all_labels)
In [100]:
#  Compute accuracy
correct = (all_preds == all_labels).sum()
test_accuracy = correct / len(all_labels)

print("\n Model Performance Summary")
print("-" * 50)
print(f"Test Accuracy: {test_accuracy:.4f}")

✅ Model Performance Summary

#  Compute Confusion Matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(all_labels, all_preds)

#  Plot Confusion Matrix
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 5))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues", xticklabels=list(sentiment_mapping.values()), yticklabels=list(sentiment_mapping.values()))
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("True Label")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix - DistilBERT Sentiment Classifier")
plt.show()
In [103]:
#  Convert labels into one-hot encoding for AUC-ROC
y_test_bin = label_binarize(all_labels, classes=range(len(sentiment_mapping)))

#  Compute ROC curve and AUC for each class
fpr = dict()
tpr = dict()
roc_auc = dict()

for i in range(len(sentiment_mapping)):
    fpr[i], tpr[i], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin[:, i], all_probs[:, i])
    roc_auc[i] = auc(fpr[i], tpr[i])

#  Compute micro-average ROC curve and AUC
fpr["micro"], tpr["micro"], _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin.ravel(), all_probs.ravel())
roc_auc["micro"] = auc(fpr["micro"], tpr["micro"])

#  Plot ROC Curves
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
for i in range(len(sentiment_mapping)):
    plt.plot(fpr[i], tpr[i], label=f'Class {sentiment_mapping[i]} (AUC = {roc_auc[i]:.2f})')

plt.plot(fpr["micro"], tpr["micro"], label=f'Micro-average (AUC = {roc_auc["micro"]:.2f})', linestyle=':', linewidth=4)
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--')  # Diagonal reference line
plt.xlim([0.0, 1.0])
plt.ylim([0.0, 1.05])
plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('ROC Curve - One vs Rest (DistilBERT)')
plt.legend(loc="lower right")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
